topics.title: History, Applications and Importance of Refrigeration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET631 - REFRIGIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** History, Applications and Importance of Refrigeration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Refrigeration

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Refrigeration** is the branch of engineering science that deals with the **production and maintenance of temperatures below that of the surrounding atmosphere** by the removal of heat from a body or space, transferring it elsewhere at a higher temperature, through the expenditure of work or energy.

According to the **Institute of Refrigeration (IOR)** and **American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers (ASHRAE)** standards adopted in the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus**:

> **Refrigeration** = Process of **removing heat** from a low-temperature medium/space and **rejecting it** to a high-temperature surrounding, using a **working fluid (refrigerant)**.

Mathematically, the basic heat balance for any refrigeration cycle is expressed as:

$$
Q_L \; + \; W_{in} \; = \; Q_H
$$

Where:
- $Q_L$ = Heat extracted from the cold space (cooling effect) — in **kW** or **kJ/s**
- $W_{in}$ = Net work input to the compressor — in **kW**
- $Q_H$ = Heat rejected at the condenser — in **kW**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of refrigeration as "pumping heat uphill."** Nature naturally lets heat flow from hot → cold (like a cup of tea cooling down). A refrigerator does the *opposite*: it **forces heat from a cold place (inside the fridge) to a warmer place (your kitchen)**, but this requires *work*, just like pumping water uphill requires a motor.

**Real-world analogy:** A refrigerator behaves like a **sponge that soaks up heat from inside** and **squeezes it out at the back**. The compressor is the "hand" squeezing the sponge, the refrigerant is the "sponge" itself, and electricity is the energy driving the hand.

## 1.3 Key Metrics and Physical Constants

| Parameter | Standard Value / Range | Significance |
|-----------|------------------------|--------------|
| **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** | **2 to 7** (typical domestic refrigerators) | Ratio of desired cooling to work input |
| **1 Ton of Refrigeration (TR)** | **3.517 kW** or **211 kJ/min** | Standard unit of cooling capacity |
| **Global Warming Potential (GWP)** | R-134a = 1430, R-410A = 2088 | Environmental impact metric |
| **Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP)** | R-11 = 1.0 (reference), R-134a = 0 | Ozone layer harm metric |
| **Absolute Zero** | **0 K = -273.15 °C = -459.67 °F** | Theoretical lower temperature limit |
| **Latent Heat of Fusion of Ice** | **334 kJ/kg** (at 0 °C) | Historical benchmark for 1 TR |

## 1.4 Distinction: Refrigeration vs. Air-Conditioning

> [!NOTE]
> **Refrigeration** primarily concerns *producing cold* for food preservation, industrial processes, and storage.
> **Air-Conditioning** involves *controlling temperature, humidity, air purity, and air motion* for human/comfort applications.
>
> Both share the **same thermodynamic vapour-compression cycle foundation** — covered in Module 2 of PEPET631.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Historical Development of Refrigeration — Structured Logic

Refrigeration evolved through **four major eras**, each driven by a scientific breakthrough:

### Era 1: Foundations (Pre-1800s)
- **Step 1 — Natural Ice Harvesting:** Ancient civilizations (Egypt ~1000 BC, India, China, Persia) used **evaporative cooling** and stored natural ice in insulated pits called *"Yakhchāls"*.
- **Step 2 — Early Compression Concepts:** In **1748**, **William Cullen** at the University of Glasgow demonstrated artificial cooling by evaporating ether under vacuum — the **first known man-made cold**.

### Era 2: Mechanical Refrigeration Birth (1800–1850)
- **Step 3 — Jacob Perkins (1834):** Built the **first closed-cycle vapour-compression refrigeration system** using ether as refrigerant. Patented in the UK.
- **Step 4 — John Gorrie (1844):** American physician who used mechanical compression to make ice for yellow-fever patients — built a working ice machine.

### Era 3: Industrial Expansion (1850–1950)
- **Step 5 — Carl von Linde (1876):** German engineer who developed the **first practical ammonia (NH₃) compression system**, founding the Linde company. This revolutionized brewing and food industries.
- **Step 6 — Willis Carrier (1902):** American engineer who invented the **first modern air-conditioning system** for a Brooklyn printing plant (humidity control). Called the **"Father of Modern Air-Conditioning."**
- **Step 7 — Synthesized Refrigerants (1930s):** **Thomas Midgley Jr.** discovered **CFC-12 (R-12)** at General Motors — non-toxic, non-flammable, and odourless. This enabled safe domestic refrigerators.

### Era 4: Environmental Awakening (1970s–Present)
- **Step 8 — Montreal Protocol (1987):** International treaty to phase out **CFCs and HCFCs** (ozone-depleting substances). KTU 2024 syllabus highlights this as a landmark.
- **Step 9 — HFC Adoption:** **R-134a, R-410A, R-32** replaced CFCs.
- **Step 10 — Green Refrigerants:** Move toward **natural refrigerants** — CO₂ (R-744), Ammonia (R-717), Hydrocarbons (R-290 propane, R-600a isobutane).

## 2.2 Why This History Matters to KTU Examiners

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently ask **2-mark and 3-mark questions** like *"Name the father of modern air-conditioning"* (Willis Carrier) or *"What is the significance of the Montreal Protocol?"* — these are direct recall items from Module 1.

## 2.3 Applications of Refrigeration — Classification

Refrigeration is the **backbone of modern civilization**. Applications are categorized as below:

### A. Domestic / Commercial Applications
- **Domestic refrigerators & freezers** for food preservation
- **Air-conditioners** (split, window, cassette, VRF systems)
- **Water coolers and ice makers** in offices and restaurants
- **Cold drink dispensers** and **beverage chilling plants**

### B. Industrial Applications
- **Food processing industries:** Dairy (milk chilling), meat packing, fish freezing, ice-cream hardening
- **Chemical & Petrochemical:** Liquefaction of gases (LNG, LPG, oxygen, nitrogen), process cooling in refineries
- **Pharmaceutical & Biotechnology:** Vaccine storage, blood banks, bio-sample preservation
- **Cold Chain Logistics:** Warehousing, refrigerated transport (reefer trucks), frozen food supply

### C. Comfort & Specialized Applications
- **Central air-conditioning** of malls, hospitals, theatres, data centres
- **Cryogenic applications** — superconducting magnets (MRI machines at **4 K = -269 °C**), particle accelerators
- **Space applications** — astronaut suits, satellite thermal control
- **Sports & recreation** — ice rinks, ski resorts
- **Electronics cooling** — server farms, supercomputers

## 2.4 Importance of Refrigeration — Why It Matters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Refrigeration is no longer a luxury — it is a critical infrastructure supporting public health, food security, and industrial progress.**

| Domain | Importance Statement |
|--------|----------------------|
| **Food Preservation** | Prevents bacterial growth (e.g., *Salmonella* thrives at 20–40 °C). Cooling to **≤ 4 °C** dramatically extends shelf-life. |
| **Public Health** | Enables safe vaccine storage (Polio, COVID-19 vaccines require **-70 °C** for mRNA types). |
| **Industrial Productivity** | Many chemical processes (polymerization, gas liquefaction) are infeasible without precise cooling. |
| **Comfort & Productivity** | AC in tropical climates like Kerala reduces heatstroke, improves office output. |
| **Scientific Research** | Cryogenics enables low-temperature physics, medical imaging, quantum computing. |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Module 1)

> [!NOTE]
> Module 1 is **qualitative & historical**, so formulas are *minimal* — but these fundamental definitions appear frequently in Part A questions across all modules.

$$
\text{COP}_{ref} \; = \; \frac{\text{Desired Cooling Effect} \, (Q_L)}{\text{Work Input} \, (W_{in})}
$$

$$
1 \; \text{TR} \; = \; \frac{211 \; \text{kJ}}{\text{min}} \; = \; 3.517 \; \text{kW}
$$

$$
Q_L \; + \; W_{in} \; = \; Q_H \quad \text{(Energy balance of any refrigerator)}
$$

$$
\text{Thermodynamic Temperature} \; (K) \; = \; °C \; + \; 273.15
$$

$$
\text{GWP} \; = \; \frac{\text{Refrigerant GHF over 100 yrs}}{\text{CO}_2 \text{ GHF over 100 yrs}}
$$

$$
\text{ODP} \; = \; \frac{\text{Refrigerant ODP}}{\text{R-11 ODP}} \; \quad (\text{R-11 baseline} = 1.0)
$$

> **Boundary Conditions to Remember:**
> - All temperatures in thermodynamic cycles use **Kelvin (K)** or **Rankine (°R)**.
> - **COP can never be less than 1** for a refrigerator (unlike engine efficiency).
> - Heat always flows **naturally** from **higher T to lower T** — refrigeration **reverses** this at the cost of work.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivations Required for Module 1 (KTU Perspective)

While Module 1 is predominantly **conceptual and historical**, the KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to derive and apply **two fundamental relations** that act as the foundation for all later modules:

### Derivation 1: Heat Balance of a Refrigerator

**Given:** A refrigeration system extracts heat $Q_L$ from a cold space, receives work input $W_{in}$, and rejects total heat $Q_H$ at the condenser.

**To Prove:** $Q_L + W_{in} = Q_H$

**Step 1 — Apply the First Law of Thermodynamics to the cyclic system:**
$$
\Delta U_{cycle} \; = \; 0 \quad \text{(since the refrigerant returns to its initial state every cycle)}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply conservation of energy between system and surroundings:**
$$
Q_{net,in} \; + \; W_{net,in} \; = \; \Delta U_{cycle}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the cyclic condition:**
$$
(Q_L - Q_H) \; + \; W_{in} \; = \; 0
$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange for the standard heat balance form:**
$$
Q_L \; + \; W_{in} \; = \; Q_H
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Heat entering the system ($Q_L$) plus work input ($W_{in}$) equals total heat rejected ($Q_H$). This is the **fundamental energy conservation law** governing every refrigeration cycle.

---

### Derivation 2: Definition of Coefficient of Performance (COP)

**Given:** Performance measure of a refrigeration system.

**To Derive:** $COP_{ref} = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{in}}$

**Step 1 — Definition (qualitative):**
A refrigerator's "goodness" is judged by **how much cooling we get per unit work spent**.

**Step 2 — Set up the ratio:**
$$
COP_{ref} \; = \; \frac{\text{Benefit}}{\text{Expenditure}} \; = \; \frac{\text{Cooling effect} \, (Q_L)}{\text{Work input} \, (W_{in})}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the heat balance $W_{in} = Q_H - Q_L$:**
$$
COP_{ref} \; = \; \frac{Q_L}{Q_H - Q_L}
$$

**Step 4 — Express alternatively using the Second Law (Carnot refrigerator):**
$$
COP_{Carnot} \; = \; \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}
$$

Where $T_L$ is the **cold space temperature in Kelvin** and $T_H$ is the **hot sink temperature in Kelvin**.

> **Conversion Logic:** The **higher the temperature lift ($T_H - T_L$)**, the **lower the COP** — meaning refrigeration becomes *harder* as the cold space gets colder or the surroundings get hotter. This is why a freezer in a Kerala summer (ambient 35 °C = 308 K) consumes more power than one in winter (ambient 20 °C = 293 K).

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently ask numerical problems where students must compute COP. Here is a model 3-mark problem.

**Problem:** A domestic refrigerator extracts **1200 kJ/h** of heat from the cold space. The compressor consumes **0.10 kW** of electrical power. Find:
- (a) Heat rejected per hour at the condenser
- (b) Coefficient of Performance

**Given Data:**
- $Q_L = 1200 \; \text{kJ/h}$
- $W_{in} = 0.10 \; \text{kW}$

**Solution:**

**Step (a) — Convert work input to kJ/h:**
$$
W_{in} \; = \; 0.10 \; \text{kW} \; \times \; 3600 \; \text{s/h} \; = \; 360 \; \text{kJ/h}
$$

**Step (b) — Apply heat balance:**
$$
Q_H \; = \; Q_L \; + \; W_{in} \; = \; 1200 \; + \; 360 \; = \; 1560 \; \text{kJ/h}
$$

**Step (c) — Compute COP:**
$$
COP_{ref} \; = \; \frac{Q_L}{W_{in}} \; = \; \frac{1200}{360} \; = \; 3.33
$$

**Answer:** $Q_H = 1560 \; \text{kJ/h}$ and $COP_{ref} = 3.33$

> **Interpretation:** This refrigerator produces **3.33 units of cooling** for every **1 unit of work** — typical for a modern domestic fridge (range 2–7).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Refrigeration Eras

| Era | Period | Key Innovation | Working Fluid | Key Figure |
|-----|--------|----------------|---------------|------------|
| Ancient | Before 1800 | Natural ice harvesting | Ice + evaporation | Egyptian/Persian engineers |
| Birth | 1800–1850 | Vapour compression prototype | Diethyl ether | Jacob Perkins (1834) |
| Industrial | 1850–1950 | Ammonia compression, AC invention | NH₃, CO₂, SO₂ | Carl von Linde, Willis Carrier |
| CFC Era | 1930–1987 | Synthetic refrigerants | R-12, R-22 | Thomas Midgley Jr. |
| Green Era | 1987–Present | Environmental phase-out | R-134a, R-410A, R-744 | Montreal Protocol signatories |
| Future | 2020–Onward | Low-GWP natural refrigerants | R-290, R-744, NH₃ | Global researchers |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS


## 4.2 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Refrigeration System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[COLD SPACE\nFood Items / Room] --> B[EVAPORATOR\nHeat Absorption]
    B --> C[COMPRESSOR\nLow P to High P Gas]
    C --> D[CONDENSER\nHeat Rejection]
    D --> E[EXPANSION VALVE\nPressure Drop]
    E --> B
    F[WORK INPUT\nElectrical Energy] --> C
    D --> G[HOT SURROUNDING\nAmbient Air]
```

**Node Descriptions:**
- **A — Cold Space:** Region to be cooled (food, room, industrial process).
- **B — Evaporator:** Refrigerant absorbs heat at low pressure/temperature.
- **C — Compressor:** Raises refrigerant pressure and temperature; consumes work.
- **D — Condenser:** Rejects heat from refrigerant to surroundings.
- **E — Expansion Valve:** Drops refrigerant pressure to start the cycle again.
- **F — Work Input:** Electrical energy supplied to compressor.
- **G — Hot Surrounding:** Ambient air/water absorbs rejected heat.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`

**Q: Define refrigeration. State the unit of refrigeration and explain its significance.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Refrigeration is the process of **removing heat from a body or space at a temperature lower than its surroundings**, and rejecting this heat to the surroundings, by using work input and a working fluid (refrigerant).

> **Unit:** The standard SI-derived unit is **Ton of Refrigeration (TR)**.
> 
> **Significance:** $1 \; \text{TR} = 3.517 \; \text{kW} = 211 \; \text{kJ/min}$, which represents the rate of heat extraction required to **melt 1 ton (1000 kg) of ice at 0 °C into water at 0 °C in 24 hours**.

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question Paper]`

**Q: List any three applications of refrigeration and explain the role of the Montreal Protocol in refrigerant history.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Applications:**
> 1. **Food preservation** in cold storages and domestic refrigerators (maintaining ≤ 4 °C).
> 2. **Air-conditioning** of buildings, hospitals, and data centres.
> 3. **Cryogenics** — liquefaction of gases like nitrogen (77 K) and oxygen (90 K).

> **Montreal Protocol (1987):** It is an **international treaty signed under the UNEP** to **phase out the production and consumption of ozone-depleting substances**, primarily **CFCs (like R-12) and HCFCs (like R-22)**. This led to the global adoption of **HFCs (R-134a, R-410A)** and later **natural refrigerants** (R-744, R-290).

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question B-A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a) Explain in detail the historical development of refrigeration, highlighting the contributions of Jacob Perkins, Carl von Linde, and Willis Carrier. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Jacob Perkins (1834):**
- American-born British inventor.
- Designed and patented the **first closed-loop vapour-compression refrigeration system**.
- Used **diethyl ether** as the working fluid.
- Although the system was not commercially viable, it laid the **theoretical and mechanical foundation** for all future VCR systems.

**Carl von Linde (1876):**
- German engineer who developed the **first practical and industrially successful ammonia (NH₃) compression refrigeration system**.
- His invention enabled **large-scale industrial refrigeration** in breweries, food processing, and cold storage.
- Founded the **Linde Company** — a pioneer in industrial gases and cryogenics.
- His contribution: bridged the gap between *theoretical concept* and *engineering reality*.

**Willis Carrier (1902):**
- American engineer, called the **"Father of Modern Air-Conditioning"**.
- Designed the **first modern air-conditioning system** for the Sackett-Wilhelms Printing Plant, Brooklyn, New York.
- His goal was to **control humidity** (not temperature) for consistent paper quality.
- This invention later spread to cinemas, offices, hospitals, and homes.
- Founded the **Carrier Corporation** in 1915.

> **Examiner's Insight:** KTU expects students to remember **names, years, and contributions** in chronological order — a 7-mark answer needs at least **three distinct paragraphs** with these elements.

---

**(b) Discuss the various applications of refrigeration in domestic, industrial, and cryogenic fields. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Domestic Applications:**
- Household refrigerators and freezers for food preservation.
- Window and split air-conditioners for human comfort.
- Water coolers and ice makers.

**Industrial Applications:**
- **Food industry:** Dairy chilling, meat freezing, ice-cream hardening, fish preservation.
- **Chemical industry:** Liquefaction of gases (LNG at -162 °C, oxygen at -183 °C), process cooling.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** Cold-chain storage of vaccines, blood banks.
- **Cold chain logistics:** Refrigerated trucks and warehouses.

**Cryogenic Applications:**
- **Medical imaging:** MRI machines using **liquid helium at 4 K**.
- **Space technology:** Cryogenic rocket fuels (liquid hydrogen at 20 K, liquid oxygen at 90 K).
- **Research:** Superconducting magnets, particle accelerators (CERN), quantum computing.
- **Electronics:** Cooling of infrared detectors and high-performance computers.

---

### Question B-B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a) Define Coefficient of Performance (COP) for a refrigerator. Derive the relation $COP = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{in}}$ and compute the COP of a refrigerator that extracts 1500 kJ/h of heat with 400 W of input power. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** Coefficient of Performance (COP) is the ratio of the **desired cooling effect ($Q_L$)** to the **work input ($W_{in}$)** required to produce that effect.

**Derivation:**
For a cyclic refrigeration system, by the **First Law of Thermodynamics**:

$$
Q_H \; - \; Q_L \; = \; W_{in}
$$

Therefore:

$$
W_{in} \; = \; Q_H \; - \; Q_L
$$

By definition:

$$
COP \; = \; \frac{Q_L}{W_{in}} \; = \; \frac{Q_L}{Q_H - Q_L}
$$

This completes the derivation.

**Numerical Computation:**
Given:
- $Q_L = 1500 \; \text{kJ/h}$
- $W_{in} = 400 \; \text{W} = 0.4 \; \text{kW} = 0.4 \times 3600 = 1440 \; \text{kJ/h}$

$$
COP \; = \; \frac{Q_L}{W_{in}} \; = \; \frac{1500}{1440} \; = \; 1.04
$$

**Note:** This is a low COP because the work input is unusually high relative to the cooling effect — perhaps an inefficient, older system.

---

**(b) Explain the importance of refrigeration in modern society with at least four specific examples. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Importance 1 — Food Security:**
Refrigeration has **revolutionized food preservation**. Cold storage extends the shelf-life of perishables from hours/days to weeks/months. The **cold chain** ensures that seafood caught in coastal Kerala reaches markets across India without spoilage, reducing post-harvest losses.

**Importance 2 — Public Health and Medicine:**
Vaccines like **Covishield (stored at 2–8 °C)** and **mRNA vaccines (stored at -70 °C)** are entirely dependent on refrigeration. Blood banks, insulin storage, and organ transport all require reliable cooling.

**Importance 3 — Industrial and Economic Growth:**
Modern industries like **petrochemicals, plastics, natural gas processing, and pharmaceuticals** depend on process cooling. Without refrigeration, the global LNG trade (worth over **$300 billion annually**) would be impossible.

**Importance 4 — Comfort and Productivity:**
In tropical regions like Kerala, air-conditioning improves **office productivity by 10–15%** during summer months. It also reduces heatstroke and improves patient recovery in hospitals.

**Importance 5 — Scientific Advancement:**
Cryogenics enables **MRI machines, superconductors, and particle accelerators**, all of which have transformed medicine and fundamental physics.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors:** Forgetting to convert W to kJ/h is a **frequent 1-mark deduction**. Always standardize units before computing COP.
> 2. **Missing chronological order:** When asked about historical figures, list them **chronologically** with their **years** — examiners reward organized answers.
> 3. **Confusing COP with efficiency:** A refrigerator's COP can be **greater than 1** (often 2–7). Saying "efficiency > 100%" is a **conceptual mistake** — COP is *not* efficiency.
> 4. **Ignoring Kelvin conversion:** Carnot COP formula requires **temperatures in Kelvin (K)**, not Celsius. Missing this loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Vague applications:** "Used in homes and industries" is too generic — name **specific systems** like "domestic refrigerator" or "MRI machine" to score full marks.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: History, Applications, and Importance of Refrigeration**

- ✅ **Refrigeration** = Removing heat from a low-temperature space and rejecting it at a higher temperature, using work input and a refrigerant.
- ✅ **1 Ton of Refrigeration (TR) = 3.517 kW = 211 kJ/min** — the standard unit of cooling capacity.
- ✅ **Heat Balance:** $Q_L + W_{in} = Q_H$ (fundamental energy balance).
- ✅ **COP of a refrigerator:** $COP = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{in}}$ (can be greater than 1).
- ✅ **Carnot COP (theoretical maximum):** $COP_{Carnot} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ (temperatures in **Kelvin**).
- ✅ **Jacob Perkins (1834)** — First vapour compression system using ether.
- ✅ **Carl von Linde (1876)** — First industrial ammonia refrigeration system.
- ✅ **Willis Carrier (1902)** — Father of modern air-conditioning.
- ✅ **Thomas Midgley Jr. (1930s)** — Invented CFC-12, later discovered to harm ozone.
- ✅ **Montreal Protocol (1987)** — International treaty to phase out CFCs/HCFCs.
- ✅ **Modern refrigerants:** R-134a, R-410A, R-32, and natural refrigerants like R-744 (CO₂) and R-290 (propane).
- ✅ **Applications span:** Domestic (fridges, ACs), Industrial (food, chemical, pharma), Cryogenic (MRI, space, superconductors).
- ✅ **Refrigeration is essential for:** Food security, public health, industrial productivity, comfort, and scientific research.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermodynamics of Refrigeration: Reversed Carnot Cycle, Heat Pumps, Limitations of the Reversed Carnot Cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET631 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Thermodynamics of Refrigeration: Reversed Carnot Cycle, Heat Pumps, Limitations of the Reversed Carnot Cycle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Reversed Carnot Cycle — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reversed Carnot Cycle**: A theoretical, idealized thermodynamic refrigeration cycle that operates in the reverse direction of the standard Carnot heat engine cycle. It consists of **two isothermal** (constant temperature) processes and **two isentropic** (constant entropy / reversible adiabatic) processes. The working fluid absorbs heat from a low-temperature reservoir (cold space) and rejects it to a high-temperature reservoir (hot space) by consuming external work input. It is the **most efficient refrigeration cycle possible** between any two given temperature limits, as dictated by the second law of thermodynamics.

In the forward (engine) direction, the Carnot cycle produces work from heat flow. When **reversed**, it consumes work to transfer heat *uphill*—from cold to hot—which is the fundamental principle behind every refrigerator, air-conditioner, and heat pump.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Refrigerator is a Heat Escalator"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy**: Imagine a water pump. A water pump cannot create water, but it can lift existing water from a low-lying pond to an overhead tank by consuming electrical energy. Similarly, a refrigerator cannot create coldness ("cooling"); it merely *pumps* heat energy from inside a cold compartment to the warmer surroundings. The work input (electricity) is the "electrical pump" lifting heat "uphill" from cold to hot against its natural flow direction.

The Reversed Carnot Cycle is the **thermodynamically perfect** version of this "heat pump." It does so with no entropy generation, meaning the maximum possible heat is moved per unit of work supplied.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Constants

- **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** — dimensionless efficiency metric, defined as:

$$COP = \frac{\text{Desired Effect}}{\text{Work Input}}$$

- **COP of a Refrigerator (COP_R)**:

$$COP_R = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}} = \frac{\text{Heat extracted from cold space}}{\text{Work input}}$$

- **COP of a Heat Pump (COP_HP)**:

$$COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{W_{net}} = \frac{\text{Heat delivered to hot space}}{\text{Work input}}$$

- **Fundamental Relationship**:

$$COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight**: For a Reversed Carnot Cycle, the COP formulas in terms of absolute reservoir temperatures are: $COP_R = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ and $COP_{HP} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$, where temperatures must be in **Kelvin (K)** or **Rankine (°R)** — *never Celsius or Fahrenheit*. This is one of the most heavily tested formula conversions in KTU exams.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Processes of the Reversed Carnot Cycle

The cycle is best understood by tracking the working fluid (refrigerant) through four distinct states on a **Pressure-Volume (P-v) diagram**, **Temperature-Entropy (T-s) diagram**, and **Pressure-Enthalpy (P-h) diagram**.

| Process | Transformation | Thermodynamic Nature | Refrigeration Component Analog |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1 → 2** | Compression | **Isentropic** (reversible adiabatic) | Compressor |
| **2 → 3** | Heat Rejection | **Isothermal** (at $T_H$, constant high temp) | Condenser |
| **3 → 4** | Expansion | **Isentropic** (reversible adiabatic) | Expansion device |
| **4 → 1** | Heat Absorption | **Isothermal** (at $T_L$, constant low temp) | Evaporator |

### Step-by-Step Logic Behind Each Process

- **Process 1-2 (Isentropic Compression):** The refrigerant vapor at low pressure and low temperature $T_L$ is compressed to a higher pressure, raising its temperature to $T_H$. Because it is isentropic, **no heat is exchanged** with surroundings—ideal for the compressor stage. Entropy remains constant: $s_1 = s_2$.

- **Process 2-3 (Isothermal Heat Rejection):** At the highest temperature $T_H$, the superheated vapor is condensed to a saturated liquid while **maintaining constant temperature** $T_H$. Heat $Q_H$ is rejected to the hot reservoir. The key insight: in a Carnot cycle, condensation occurs **at constant temperature**, requiring a perfect isothermal heat sink.

- **Process 3-4 (Isentropic Expansion):** The high-pressure saturated liquid is expanded (ideally, through a turbine doing useful work, or practically through a throttling valve) to the low-pressure side, with **no heat exchange**. Entropy remains constant: $s_3 = s_4$. Temperature drops from $T_H$ to $T_L$.

- **Process 4-1 (Isothermal Heat Absorption):** At the low temperature $T_L$, the liquid-vapor mixture evaporates while absorbing heat $Q_L$ from the cold reservoir (the space to be refrigerated). Temperature is held constant at $T_L$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Reversed"?** If the cycle traversed 1→4→3→2→1 instead, it would be a heat engine producing work from heat. Reversing it means work is *consumed* to move heat from cold to hot.

## 2.2 Energy Balance (First Law Applied to the Cycle)

The net work input is the difference between heat rejected and heat absorbed:

$$W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L$$

This relationship holds universally for any reversed cycle. For the Carnot (reversible) case, applying the second law gives us the relationship between heat and absolute temperature:

$$\frac{Q_H}{T_H} = \frac{Q_L}{T_L} \quad \text{(Carnot reversibility condition)}$$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Reversed Carnot Cycle is the **theoretical benchmark** (the "gold standard") against which **all practical refrigeration cycles** — vapor compression, vapor absorption, air refrigeration, etc. — are compared. No real system can match it, but engineering aims to approach it. In design:

- Aerospace and cryogenic applications use **reverse Carnot** concepts with regenerators and multi-staging.
- Heat pumps for residential heating in cold climates (e.g., Scandinavian countries) are evaluated using **COP_HP** as the primary performance metric.
- The relationship $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$ tells engineers that any device that is "good at cooling" is *even better at heating*, which is why modern reversible heat pumps are favored over furnaces.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of COP of Reversed Carnot Refrigerator

### Starting Point — Energy Balance (First Law of Thermodynamics)

For a cyclic device, the net work input equals the net heat transfer over one complete cycle. The refrigerator extracts heat $Q_L$ from the cold reservoir and rejects heat $Q_H$ to the hot reservoir, with work $W_{net}$ supplied from outside.

By the **First Law applied to a cycle** (no change in internal energy over one complete cycle):

$$W_{net,in} = Q_H - Q_L$$

This is the algebraic form of energy conservation: work in = heat out − heat in.

### Definition of COP for a Refrigerator

By definition, the desired effect of a refrigerator is the heat removed from the cold space, $Q_L$. So:

$$COP_R = \frac{\text{Desired Effect}}{\text{Work Input}} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}}$$

Substituting the energy balance:

$$COP_R = \frac{Q_L}{Q_H - Q_L}$$

### Second Law Constraint (Reversibility)

For a **reversible (Carnot) cycle**, the **Clausius equality** must hold — the cyclic integral of $\dfrac{\delta Q}{T}$ equals zero. This gives:

$$\frac{Q_H}{T_H} = \frac{Q_L}{T_L}$$

Rearranging to express $Q_H$ in terms of $Q_L$:

$$Q_H = Q_L \cdot \frac{T_H}{T_L}$$

### Substituting into the COP Expression

Insert $Q_H$ into the COP_R expression:

$$COP_R = \frac{Q_L}{Q_L \cdot \dfrac{T_H}{T_L} - Q_L}$$

Factor $Q_L$ out of the denominator:

$$COP_R = \frac{Q_L}{Q_L \left( \dfrac{T_H}{T_L} - 1 \right)}$$

Cancel $Q_L$ from numerator and denominator:

$$COP_R = \frac{1}{\dfrac{T_H}{T_L} - 1}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $T_L$:

$$COP_R = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Result:** $\boxed{COP_R = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}}$
>
> This is the **maximum possible COP** for any refrigerator operating between reservoirs at $T_L$ and $T_H$. No real refrigerator can exceed this value.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of COP of Reversed Carnot Heat Pump

### Definition of COP for a Heat Pump

For a heat pump, the desired effect is the **heat delivered to the hot space**, $Q_H$ (used for space heating or water heating). So:

$$COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{W_{net}} = \frac{Q_H}{Q_H - Q_L}$$

### Applying the Reversibility Condition

Using $\dfrac{Q_H}{Q_L} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_L}$, we have $Q_L = Q_H \cdot \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$. Substituting:

$$COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{Q_H - Q_H \cdot \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}}$$

Factor $Q_H$:

$$COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{Q_H \left( 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H} \right)}$$

Cancel $Q_H$:

$$COP_{HP} = \frac{1}{1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $T_H$:

$$COP_{HP} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Result:** $\boxed{COP_{HP} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}}$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Relationship $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$

Start with the two derived COPs:

$$COP_R = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} \quad \text{and} \quad COP_{HP} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$$

Compute $COP_R + 1$:

$$COP_R + 1 = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} + 1 = \frac{T_L + (T_H - T_L)}{T_H - T_L}$$

Simplify the numerator: $T_L + T_H - T_L = T_H$:

$$COP_R + 1 = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$$

But $\dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$ is exactly $COP_{HP}$:

$$\boxed{COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip**: This relationship is **universally true** for any refrigeration cycle, not just Carnot. It can be derived directly from the energy balance: $COP_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W} = \dfrac{Q_L + W}{W} = \dfrac{Q_L}{W} + 1 = COP_R + 1$.

---

## 3.4 Sample Worked Numerical — KTU 14-Mark Style

### Problem Statement

A reversed Carnot refrigerator operates between a cold space at **$-10^{\circ}C$** and a hot space at **$40^{\circ}C$**. The heat extraction rate is **$5 \text{ TR}$** (tons of refrigeration).

**Required:**
1. Calculate the COP of the refrigerator and the heat pump.
2. Determine the power input to the compressor in kW.
3. Calculate the rate of heat rejection to the hot space.

### Step 1 — Convert Temperatures to Kelvin

$$T_L = -10 + 273.15 = 263.15 \text{ K} \quad \text{(use 263 K for exam-style)}$$ 

$$T_H = 40 + 273.15 = 313.15 \text{ K} \quad \text{(use 313 K for exam-style)}$$

> **Exam Tip:** KTU solutions typically use $T(K) = T(^{\circ}C) + 273$ for simplicity.

### Step 2 — Calculate COP of the Refrigerator

$$COP_R = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{263}{313 - 263} = \frac{263}{50} = 5.26$$

### Step 3 — Calculate COP of the Heat Pump

$$COP_{HP} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{313}{50} = 6.26$$

**Verification using the relationship:**

$$COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1 = 5.26 + 1 = 6.26 \quad \checkmark$$

### Step 4 — Convert 5 TR to kW (Refrigeration Effect)

$$Q_L = 5 \text{ TR} \times 3.517 \text{ kW/TR} = 17.585 \text{ kW}$$

### Step 5 — Calculate the Power Input

$$W_{net} = \frac{Q_L}{COP_R} = \frac{17.585}{5.26} = 3.343 \text{ kW}$$

### Step 6 — Calculate the Heat Rejection Rate

$$Q_H = Q_L + W_{net} = 17.585 + 3.343 = 20.928 \text{ kW}$$

Or, equivalently:

$$Q_H = W_{net} \times COP_{HP} = 3.343 \times 6.26 = 20.928 \text{ kW} \quad \checkmark$$

### Final Answer Summary

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| $COP_R$ | **5.26** |
| $COP_{HP}$ | **6.26** |
| Power Input $W_{net}$ | **3.343 kW** |
| Heat Rejection $Q_H$ | **20.928 kW** |

---

## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Parameter | Reversed Carnot Cycle | Practical Vapour Compression Cycle |
|---|---|---|
| Processes | 2 Isothermal + 2 Isentropic | 2 Isobaric + 1 Isentropic + 1 Throttling |
| Compression | Isentropic (wet compression possible) | Isentropic (dry/superheated compression only) |
| Expansion | Isentropic (turbine) | Throttling (irreversible) |
| Realizability | Theoretical only | Industrially standard |
| COP | **Maximum possible** | Lower (60-80% of Carnot) |
| Reversibility | Fully reversible | Contains internal irreversibility |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Reversed Carnot Cycle on T-s Diagram (Mermaid Block-Level Schematic)

The Temperature-Entropy (T-s) diagram is the **canonical representation** of the Reversed Carnot Cycle because the cycle traces a clean rectangle on it — directly showing the two isotherms and two isentropes.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    state1["STATE 1: Saturated vapor at low temp T_L and high entropy s1"]
    state2["STATE 2: Superheated vapor at high temp T_H and same entropy s1 equal to s2"]
    state3["STATE 3: Saturated liquid at high temp T_H and low entropy s3"]
    state4["STATE 4: Liquid-vapor mix at low temp T_L and same entropy s3 equal to s4"]

    proc12["Process 1 to 2: ISENTROPIC COMPRESSION, W in, T rises from T_L to T_H"]
    proc23["Process 2 to 3: ISOTHERMAL HEAT REJECTION, Q_H out, T constant at T_H"]
    proc34["Process 3 to 4: ISENTROPIC EXPANSION, W out, T falls from T_H to T_L"]
    proc41["Process 4 to 1: ISOTHERMAL HEAT ABSORPTION, Q_L in, T constant at T_L"]

    state1 --> proc12 --> state2
    state2 --> proc23 --> state3
    state3 --> proc34 --> state4
    state4 --> proc41 --> state1

    hotRes["HOT RESERVOIR at T_H, receives Q_H"]
    coldRes["COLD RESERVOIR at T_L, supplies Q_L"]
    workIn["WORK SOURCE, supplies W_net"]

    proc23 -. heat Q_H .-> hotRes
    coldRes -. heat Q_L .-> proc41
    workIn -. work W .-> proc12
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for Students:** On a real T-s plot, the cycle is a **clockwise rectangle** (when viewed with T on the y-axis and s on the x-axis). The two horizontal lines (top at $T_H$, bottom at $T_L$) are the isothermal processes; the two vertical lines (left and right) are the isentropic processes. The area enclosed represents the **net work input** of the cycle.

## 4.2 Reversed Carnot Cycle on P-v Diagram (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pvA["State 1: LOW PRESSURE P_L, LOW TEMP T_L"]
    pvB["State 2: HIGH PRESSURE P_H, HIGH TEMP T_H"]
    pvC["State 3: HIGH PRESSURE P_H, SAT LIQUID at T_H"]
    pvD["State 4: LOW PRESSURE P_L, MIX at T_L"]

    isoCompress["ISENTROPIC COMPRESSION 1 to 2, curve rises"]
    isoCondense["ISOTHERMAL CONDENSATION 2 to 3, horizontal pressure line"]
    isoExpand["ISENTROPIC EXPANSION 3 to 4, curve drops"]
    isoEvap["ISOTHERMAL EVAPORATION 4 to 1, horizontal pressure line"]

    pvA --> isoCompress --> pvB
    pvB --> isoCondense --> pvC
    pvC --> isoExpand --> pvD
    pvD --> isoEvap --> pvA
```

> [!NOTE]
> On the **P-v diagram**, isothermal and isentropic curves are *not straight lines* but hyperbolic-like curves. The cycle is **not a rectangle** on P-v — only on T-s. This distinction is **a common KTU question**.

## 4.3 Refrigeration System Architecture Block Diagram

This shows the physical hardware that **approximates** the Reversed Carnot Cycle:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    coldSpace["COLD SPACE TO BE COOLED, T_L"]
    evaporator["EVAPORATOR, absorbs Q_L from cold space"]
    compressor["COMPRESSOR, consumes W_in, raises pressure and temperature"]
    condenser["CONDENSER, rejects Q_H to surroundings"]
    expansionValve["EXPANSION VALVE, drops pressure to P_L"]

    coldSpace --> evaporator
    evaporator --> compressor
    compressor --> condenser
    condenser --> expansionValve
    expansionValve --> evaporator
```

**Mapping to Reversed Carnot Process:**

| Real Component | Reversed Carnot Process |
|---|---|
| Evaporator | Process 4 → 1 (Isothermal heat absorption) |
| Compressor | Process 1 → 2 (Isentropic compression) |
| Condenser | Process 2 → 3 (Isothermal heat rejection) |
| Expansion Valve | Process 3 → 4 (Isentropic expansion — approximated, not exact) |

## 4.4 Heat Pump Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    outdoorAir["OUTDOOR AIR, Low T source, rejects no heat, gives up heat Q_L"]
    outdoorGround["GROUND or WATER SOURCE, alternative low T reservoir"]
    evapHP["EVAPORATOR of HEAT PUMP, absorbs Q_L from outdoor air or ground"]
    compHP["COMPRESSOR, electrical W_in"]
    condHP["CONDENSER of HEAT PUMP, delivers useful heat Q_H to indoor space"]
    indoorSpace["INDOOR SPACE, heated to comfort temperature"]

    outdoorAir -. Q_L .-> evapHP
    outdoorGround -. Q_L .-> evapHP
    evapHP --> compHP --> condHP --> indoorSpace
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight**: A heat pump and a refrigerator are **physically the same device** — only the *desired output* differs. In a refrigerator, the useful output is $Q_L$ (cooling). In a heat pump, the useful output is $Q_H$ (heating). The condenser is *inside* the room for a heat pump, but *outside* for an A.C./refrigerator.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern**: Part A questions are 2-mark or 3-mark short answer/definition type. They test direct recall, definitions, and one-line derivations. Word limit: 80-100 words.

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]**
**Define the Coefficient of Performance (COP) of a refrigerator and a heat pump. Show that $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$.**

**Model Answer:**

The COP of any refrigeration/heat pump device is defined as the ratio of the desired effect to the work input.

$$COP_R = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}} \quad ; \quad COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{W_{net}}$$

By the first law, $Q_H = Q_L + W_{net}$, hence:

$$COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_L + W_{net}}{W_{net}} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}} + 1 = COP_R + 1 \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Most students write the first law but forget the final substitution step. Don't lose that 1 mark.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]**
**A reversed Carnot cycle operates between $-20^{\circ}C$ and $40^{\circ}C$. Calculate the COP of the refrigerator and the heat pump.**

**Model Answer:**

Converting to Kelvin:

$$T_L = -20 + 273 = 253 \text{ K}; \quad T_H = 40 + 273 = 313 \text{ K}$$

$$COP_R = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{253}{313 - 253} = \frac{253}{60} = 4.217$$

$$COP_{HP} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{313}{60} = 5.217$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Pitfall:** A common mistake is using Celsius temperatures directly. **Always convert to Kelvin** for thermodynamic COP calculations. Also, do not confuse "−20" as 20; the negative sign matters.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern**: Each Part B question is 14 marks, typically with internal choice. We provide **Question A** and **Question B** as alternatives. Each has sub-parts (a) and (b), each worth 7 marks.

### 📘 Question A — **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 1, Q1]**

**A reversed Carnot refrigerator is used to maintain a space at $-5^{\circ}C$ when the ambient temperature is $35^{\circ}C$. The refrigeration effect required is $10 \text{ kW}$.**

#### (a) **[7 Marks]** Determine: (i) COP of the system, (ii) Power input to the compressor, (iii) Heat rejected to the surroundings.

#### (b) **[7 Marks]** If the same system is used as a heat pump for heating purposes, determine: (i) COP of the heat pump, (ii) Heat delivered to the indoor space, (iii) Comment on the difference between the two COPs.

---

**Model Solution for Q-A:**

**Part (a) — Refrigerator Mode:**

**Step 1 — Convert Temperatures to Kelvin:**

$$T_L = -5 + 273 = 268 \text{ K}$$

$$T_H = 35 + 273 = 308 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2 — COP of Reversed Carnot Refrigerator:**

$$COP_R = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{268}{308 - 268} = \frac{268}{40} = 6.70$$

**Step 3 — Power Input to Compressor:**

Given: Refrigeration effect $Q_L = 10 \text{ kW}$. Since $COP_R = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net}}$:

$$W_{net} = \frac{Q_L}{COP_R} = \frac{10}{6.70} = 1.493 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — Heat Rejected to Surroundings:**

By first law energy balance:

$$Q_H = Q_L + W_{net} = 10 + 1.493 = 11.493 \text{ kW}$$

---

**Part (b) — Heat Pump Mode:**

**Step 1 — COP of Heat Pump (using the relationship):**

$$COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1 = 6.70 + 1 = 7.70$$

Or, directly:

$$COP_{HP} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{308}{40} = 7.70$$

**Step 2 — Heat Delivered to Indoor Space:**

For a heat pump, the useful output is $Q_H$. Using $COP_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W_{net}}$:

$$Q_H = COP_{HP} \times W_{net} = 7.70 \times 1.493 = 11.5 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — Comment on the Difference:**

The COP of the heat pump (7.70) is greater than the COP of the refrigerator (6.70) by exactly 1. This is because the heat pump delivers **both** the heat extracted from outside ($Q_L = 10 \text{ kW}$) **and** the work converted to heat ($W_{net} = 1.493 \text{ kW}$) to the indoor space, whereas the refrigerator discards $Q_H$ to the surroundings as waste. For heating applications, a heat pump is therefore more efficient than direct electric heating (which would give COP = 1).

---

### 📗 Question B — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 1, Q2]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat P-v and T-s diagram, explain the working of a reversed Carnot cycle. List any **three limitations** of this cycle.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A reversed Carnot heat pump is used to heat a building. The inside temperature is to be maintained at $25^{\circ}C$ when the outside temperature is $5^{\circ}C$. If the heat loss from the building is $50 \text{ kW}$, determine: (i) COP of the heat pump, (ii) Power input, (iii) Rate of heat extracted from outside air.

---

**Model Solution for Q-B:**

**Part (a) — Explanation and Diagrams:**

**Working of Reversed Carnot Cycle:**

The reversed Carnot cycle is the most efficient refrigeration cycle operating between two temperature reservoirs $T_L$ (cold) and $T_H$ (hot). The working fluid undergoes four reversible processes:

- **Process 1-2 (Isentropic Compression):** The refrigerant vapor at low pressure and temperature $T_L$ is compressed isentropically to a higher pressure, with temperature rising to $T_H$. No heat transfer occurs. The compressor supplies work $W_{in}$.

- **Process 2-3 (Isothermal Heat Rejection):** At constant high temperature $T_H$, the superheated vapor is condensed to saturated liquid, rejecting heat $Q_H$ to the hot reservoir (surroundings).

- **Process 3-4 (Isentropic Expansion):** The high-pressure liquid expands isentropically to low pressure. Its temperature drops from $T_H$ to $T_L$. No heat is exchanged.

- **Process 4-1 (Isothermal Heat Absorption):** At constant low temperature $T_L$, the refrigerant absorbs heat $Q_L$ from the cold space and evaporates fully to saturated vapor.

The cycle is traversed **clockwise on T-s** and **counter-clockwise on P-v** (when read as a heat engine), consuming net work $W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L$.

**Three Limitations of Reversed Carnot Cycle:**

1. **Isentropic compression of wet vapor is impractical.** The cycle requires compression starting from state 1, which lies inside the vapor dome (wet vapor region). Real reciprocating compressors cannot efficiently compress wet vapor due to liquid slugging and cylinder damage. Practical cycles always use dry/superheated compression.

2. **Isothermal heat transfer during condensation and evaporation is unattainable.** True isothermal heat rejection/absorption would require the refrigerant to remain at $T_H$ / $T_L$ even as it changes phase, but real heat exchangers have a finite temperature difference (LMTD ≠ 0), causing irreversibility.

3. **Isentropic expansion via a turbine is impractical.** The cycle mandates a work-extracting turbine (or ideal isentropic expansion). In practice, only a throttling valve is used, which is highly irreversible and reduces COP significantly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Additional Limitations (For Extra Credit):**
> - Very high compressor discharge pressure leads to heavy equipment.
> - Very low COP when temperature lift is large.
> - Not suitable for aircraft applications requiring compactness.
> - Compression of vapor to very high pressure is energy-intensive.

---

**Part (b) — Heat Pump Calculation:**

**Step 1 — Convert Temperatures to Kelvin:**

$$T_H = 25 + 273 = 298 \text{ K (inside)}$$

$$T_L = 5 + 273 = 278 \text{ K (outside)}$$

**Step 2 — COP of Heat Pump:**

$$COP_{HP} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{298}{298 - 278} = \frac{298}{20} = 14.90$$

**Step 3 — Power Input:**

The heat loss from the building is the **heat that must be supplied** to maintain inside temperature, so $Q_H = 50 \text{ kW}$.

$$W_{net} = \frac{Q_H}{COP_{HP}} = \frac{50}{14.90} = 3.356 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — Rate of Heat Extracted from Outside Air:**

By first law: $Q_L = Q_H - W_{net}$:

$$Q_L = 50 - 3.356 = 46.644 \text{ kW}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Observation:** The heat pump delivers 50 kW of heating using only 3.356 kW of electrical input — the remaining 46.644 kW is "free" heat extracted from outdoor air. This is why heat pumps are 4-5 times more efficient than electric resistance heaters.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams**
>
> 1. **Temperature Unit Error (2-3 marks lost):** Using Celsius in $COP = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$. **Always convert to Kelvin first** — even a 1-mark sub-question requires this.
>
> 2. **Sign Confusion in Heat Pump vs. Refrigerator (3-4 marks lost):** $Q_L$ is the *useful* output for a refrigerator; $Q_H$ is the *useful* output for a heat pump. Students often compute $Q_L$ when the question asks for heat delivered to a building.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$ shortcut:** Computing $COP_{HP}$ from scratch instead of using this relationship wastes 2-3 minutes — costly in a time-pressured exam.
>
> 4. **Unit Confusion in Tons of Refrigeration:** 1 TR = **3.517 kW** (not 3.5 or 4.0). Memorize this to 3 decimal places.
>
> 5. **Mixing Up "Reversed Carnot" and "Carnot Heat Engine":** The COP of a refrigerator and the efficiency of a Carnot engine are *not* the same formula. The Carnot engine efficiency is $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$, while the Carnot refrigerator COP is $COP_R = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$. **KTU frequently tests this distinction.**
>
> 6. **Skipping the Diagram:** In 7-mark questions on cycle explanation, **always draw both T-s and P-v diagrams** (even sketches). A missing diagram typically costs 1-2 marks.
>
> 7. **Stating the Relationship Without Deriving It:** If asked to "show that," partial credit is given for the derivation steps. A bare final answer with no working gets zero.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam**

### Core Concepts
- The **Reversed Carnot Cycle** is the *theoretically perfect* refrigeration cycle with maximum possible COP.
- It consists of **2 isothermal** + **2 isentropic** processes — a rectangle on the **T-s diagram**.
- The cycle is traversed in the direction that **consumes work** to move heat from cold to hot.

### Must-Know Formulas
- $COP_R = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net}} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ (use Kelvin!)
- $COP_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W_{net}} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$ (use Kelvin!)
- $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$ (universally true for any cycle)
- $W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L$ (First Law energy balance)
- $1 \text{ TR} = 3.517 \text{ kW}$ (essential unit conversion)

### Heat Pump vs. Refrigerator
- **Same hardware, different desired outputs.**
- **Refrigerator** wants $Q_L$ (cooling effect).
- **Heat pump** wants $Q_H$ (heating effect).
- $COP_{HP} > COP_R$ always.

### Limitations of Reversed Carnot Cycle (Top 5)
1. Wet vapor compression is impractical in real compressors.
2. True isothermal heat transfer is not achievable with finite-area heat exchangers.
3. Isentropic expansion (turbine) is replaced by throttling valve — irreversible.
4. Compression to very high pressures requires heavy, expensive equipment.
5. Very low COP when the temperature difference $(T_H - T_L)$ is large.

### Practical vs. Theoretical
- All real cycles have **COP < Carnot COP**.
- Goal of refrigeration engineering: design practical cycles (vapor compression, absorption) that **approach** the Carnot COP.
- Typical real-world COP is 60-80% of the Carnot value.

### Exam-Ready Diagram Knowledge
- **T-s diagram**: rectangle (isotherms horizontal, isentropes vertical).
- **P-v diagram**: non-rectangular loop (curved isentropes, horizontal isotherms).
- **P-h diagram**: not a closed loop for Carnot (used mainly for vapor compression cycles).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Units of Refrigeration: Definition and Practical Examples
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET631 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Units of Refrigeration: Definition and Practical Examples

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition of Refrigeration Effect
The **Refrigeration Effect (RE)** is the quantity of heat removed from the refrigerated space per unit time. It quantifies the *cooling capacity* of a refrigeration system and forms the basis for expressing capacity in standardized **Units of Refrigeration**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> *The rate of heat extraction from a body or space to lower its temperature below that of its surroundings is termed the **Refrigeration Effect (RE)**. It is measured in standard units of refrigeration, the most common being the **Ton of Refrigeration (TR)**.*

### 1.2 Why Do We Need "Units of Refrigeration"?
Just as electrical power is expressed in **Watts (W)** or **Kilowatts (kW)** and mechanical work in **Newton-metres (Nm)**, refrigeration systems also require a standard unit to specify their cooling capacity. Without a standardized unit, comparing a household refrigerator to an industrial cold-storage plant would be impossible.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Ice Factory Analogy 🧊**
> Imagine a small ice factory from the early 1900s. The factory's capacity was described as *"how much ice it could produce in a day"*. If a factory could freeze **1 ton of water at 0°C into ice at 0°C in 24 hours**, that factory had a capacity of **1 Ton of Refrigeration (1 TR)**. Although modern systems do not actually make ice, they are still rated in TR for the sake of this historical, standardised comparison.

### 1.4 The Five Standard Units of Refrigeration
The following units are recognised by KTU/IS units and are used interchangeably:

| Unit Symbol | Unit Name | Use Case |
|-------------|-----------|----------|
| **TR** | Ton of Refrigeration | Universal commercial standard |
| **kW** | Kilowatt | SI unit, used in engineering practice |
| **kJ/s** | Kilojoule per second | SI base form of kW |
| **kcal/h** | Kilocalorie per hour | Used in CGS/Metric systems |
| **BTU/h** | British Thermal Unit per hour | Imperial system (US, UK) |
| **HP** | Horsepower | Used for compressor motor rating |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners expect students to be fluent in converting between **TR, kW, kcal/h, BTU/h, BTU/min, and HP**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Ton of Refrigeration (TR) — The Master Unit
A **Ton of Refrigeration (TR)** is the standard commercial unit of refrigeration effect. It is defined as the rate of heat extraction required to freeze **1 ton (2000 lb or 907.185 kg)** of pure water at **0 °C** into ice at **0 °C** in **24 hours**.

The underlying physics uses the **latent heat of fusion of ice**:
- Latent heat of fusion, $L_f = 335 \text{ kJ/kg}$ (or 79.6 kcal/kg, or 144 BTU/lb)
- Mass to be frozen, $m = 1 \text{ ton} = 907.185 \text{ kg}$
- Time available, $t = 24 \text{ hours} = 86400 \text{ seconds}$

### 2.2 Stepwise Logic Behind the Numerical Value of 1 TR
- **Step 1:** Total heat to be removed in 24 hours
  $$Q_{24} = m \times L_f = 907.185 \times 335 \text{ kJ}$$
- **Step 2:** Convert to per-hour rate
  $$Q_{per\,hour} = \frac{907.185 \times 335}{24} \text{ kJ/h}$$
- **Step 3:** Convert to per-second rate (kW)
  $$1 \text{ TR} = \frac{907.185 \times 335}{24 \times 3600} \text{ kW} \approx 3.517 \text{ kW}$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Symbol | Numerical Value | Engineering Use |
|----------|--------|-----------------|------------------|
| $1 \text{ TR}$ in kW | $\text{TR} \to \text{kW}$ | $\mathbf{3.517 \text{ kW}}$ | SI standard |
| $1 \text{ TR}$ in kJ/h | $\text{TR} \to \text{kJ/h}$ | $\mathbf{12665.4 \text{ kJ/h}}$ | Heat load calculations |
| $1 \text{ TR}$ in kcal/h | $\text{TR} \to \text{kcal/h}$ | $\mathbf{3024.4 \text{ kcal/h}}$ | Metric system |
| $1 \text{ TR}$ in kcal/min | $\text{TR} \to \text{kcal/min}$ | $\mathbf{50.4 \text{ kcal/min}}$ | Short interval problems |
| $1 \text{ TR}$ in BTU/h | $\text{TR} \to \text{BTU/h}$ | $\mathbf{12000 \text{ BTU/h}}$ (exact) | Imperial (US/UK) |
| $1 \text{ TR}$ in BTU/min | $\text{TR} \to \text{BTU/min}$ | $\mathbf{200 \text{ BTU/min}}$ (exact) | Imperial rapid calc. |
| $1 \text{ TR}$ in BTU/s | $\text{TR} \to \text{BTU/s}$ | $\mathbf{3.333 \text{ BTU/s}}$ | US compressor ratings |
| $1 \text{ TR}$ in HP | $\text{TR} \to \text{HP}$ | $\mathbf{4.716 \text{ HP}}$ | Compressor motor size |
| $1 \text{ kW}$ in TR | $\text{kW} \to \text{TR}$ | $\mathbf{0.2843 \text{ TR}}$ | Reverse conversion |
| $1 \text{ kcal/h}$ in TR | $\text{kcal/h} \to \text{TR}$ | $\mathbf{3.306 \times 10^{-4} \text{ TR}}$ | Metric-to-TR |
| $1 \text{ BTU/h}$ in TR | $\text{BTU/h} \to \text{TR}$ | $\mathbf{8.33 \times 10^{-5} \text{ TR}}$ | Imperial-to-TR |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick Mnemonic for KTU Exams:**
> *"1 TR = 3.5 kW ≈ 200 BTU/min ≈ 12000 BTU/h ≈ 50 kcal/min"*
> Memorize this line; it solves 90 % of the conversion problems in Part A.

### 2.4 Other Auxiliary Units

**Kilowatt (kW):** The SI unit of power.
$$1 \text{ kW} = 1 \text{ kJ/s} = 1000 \text{ J/s}$$

**Kilocalorie per hour (kcal/h):** A metric unit. 1 kcal = 4.186 kJ.
$$1 \text{ kcal/h} = \frac{4.186}{3600} \text{ kW} = 1.163 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kW}$$

**British Thermal Unit per hour (BTU/h):** Imperial unit. 1 BTU = 1055.06 J.
$$1 \text{ BTU/h} = 0.29307 \text{ W}$$

**Horsepower (HP):** Mechanical unit. 1 HP = 745.7 W.
$$1 \text{ HP} = 0.7457 \text{ kW}$$

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **HVAC Design:** Load estimation for buildings is done in **TR** (e.g., 1.5 TR split AC, 50 TR chiller).
- **Cold Storage:** Capacity rated in **TR** for the entire plant.
- **Refrigerant & Compressor Sizing:** Calculated using **kW** or **HP** for compressor work.
- **International Equipment Datasheets:** Use **BTU/h** in the US and **kcal/h** in Europe/Asia.
- **Energy Auditing:** A common KPI is **kW/TR** — *energy consumed per ton of refrigeration*, indicating system efficiency.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of 1 TR in Standard SI Units

We start from the formal definition:
> "1 TR is the rate of heat removal required to freeze 1 ton (2000 lb) of water at 0 °C into ice at 0 °C in 24 hours."

**Step 1 — Identify the mass in SI (kg):**
$$1 \text{ ton (short ton, US)} = 2000 \text{ lb}$$
$$1 \text{ lb} = 0.4536 \text{ kg}$$
$$\therefore 1 \text{ ton} = 2000 \times 0.4536 = 907.185 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the latent heat of fusion of water at 0 °C:**
$$L_f = 335 \text{ kJ/kg} \quad \text{(or 79.6 kcal/kg, or 144 BTU/lb)}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the total heat removed in 24 hours (kJ):**
$$Q_{24} = m \times L_f$$
$$Q_{24} = 907.185 \text{ kg} \times 335 \text{ kJ/kg}$$
$$Q_{24} = 303907.0 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to heat removed per hour (kJ/h):**
$$Q_{hour} = \frac{Q_{24}}{24 \text{ h}}$$
$$Q_{hour} = \frac{303907.0}{24}$$
$$Q_{hour} = 12662.8 \text{ kJ/h}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to kilowatts (kJ/s):**
$$1 \text{ TR} = \frac{12662.8 \text{ kJ/h}}{3600 \text{ s/h}}$$
$$1 \text{ TR} = 3.517 \text{ kJ/s} = 3.517 \text{ kW}$$

This is the canonical value used in KTU 2024 scheme textbooks.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of 1 TR in kcal/h

Using 1 kcal = 4.186 kJ:
$$1 \text{ TR in kJ/h} = 12662.8 \text{ kJ/h}$$
$$1 \text{ TR in kcal/h} = \frac{12662.8}{4.186}$$
$$1 \text{ TR} = 3024.4 \text{ kcal/h} \approx 3024 \text{ kcal/h}$$

Equivalently:
$$1 \text{ TR} = \frac{3024.4}{60} \text{ kcal/min} = 50.4 \text{ kcal/min}$$

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation of 1 TR in BTU/h

Using the imperial definition directly:
- 1 ton = 2000 lb
- $L_f = 144 \text{ BTU/lb}$
- Total heat in 24 h = $2000 \times 144 = 288000 \text{ BTU}$
- Per hour = $\frac{288000}{24} = 12000 \text{ BTU/h}$ (exact)
- Per minute = $\frac{12000}{60} = 200 \text{ BTU/min}$ (exact)

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Demonstration

**Problem:** A domestic refrigerator extracts 700 kJ of heat in 5 minutes. What is its capacity in TR, kW, and BTU/h?

**Solution:**
$$\text{Heat extracted} = 700 \text{ kJ} \text{ in } 5 \text{ min} = 300 \text{ s}$$
$$\text{Capacity in kW} = \frac{700 \text{ kJ}}{300 \text{ s}} = 2.333 \text{ kW}$$

Converting to TR:
$$\text{Capacity in TR} = \frac{2.333}{3.517} = 0.663 \text{ TR}$$

Converting to BTU/h:
$$\text{Capacity in BTU/h} = 0.663 \times 12000 = 7958 \text{ BTU/h}$$

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Unit | Equivalent Heat Removed in 24 h | Equivalent Heat per Hour | Equivalent in kW |
|------|----------------------------------|---------------------------|------------------|
| 1 TR | 303907 kJ | 12662.8 kJ/h | 3.517 kW |
| 1 kW | — | 3600 kJ/h | 1 kW |
| 1 kcal/h | — | 4.186 kJ/h | 0.001163 kW |
| 1 BTU/h | — | 1.055 kJ/h | 0.000293 kW |
| 1 HP | — | 2684.5 kJ/h | 0.7457 kW |

### 3.6 Practical Examples of Capacity Ranges (KTU Real-World Context)

| Application | Typical Range (TR) | Typical Range (kW) |
|-------------|---------------------|---------------------|
| Domestic Refrigerator | 0.05 – 0.3 | 0.18 – 1.05 |
| Window Air Conditioner | 1.0 – 2.0 | 3.5 – 7.0 |
| Split AC (1.5 TR – common) | 1.0 – 2.5 | 3.5 – 8.8 |
| Commercial Deep Freezer | 0.5 – 2.0 | 1.75 – 7.0 |
| Cold Storage Plant (small) | 5 – 20 | 17.5 – 70 |
| Cold Storage Plant (large) | 50 – 200 | 175 – 700 |
| Ice Skating Rink | 100 – 400 | 350 – 1400 |
| District Cooling Plant | 1000 – 5000+ | 3500 – 17500+ |
| LNG Liquefaction | 5000 – 50000+ | 17500 – 175000+ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
