| Parameter | HEPA Filter | ULPA Filter |
|---|---|---|
| Full form | High-Efficiency Particulate Air | Ultra-Low Penetration Air |
| Test particle size | 0.3 μm (MPPS) | 0.1–0.2 μm (MPPS) |
| Minimum efficiency | 99.97% | 99.9995% |
| Typical ΔP | 25–50 mm WC | 50–75 mm WC |
| Application | Hospitals, OTs, pharma | Semi-conductor fabs, nuclear |

*(3 marks: 1 each for efficiency, particle size, application)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Define Bypass Factor of a cooling coil. What is its significance?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Bypass Factor (BPF)** of a cooling coil is defined as the ratio of the temperature difference between leaving air and entering air to the temperature difference between entering air and the Apparatus Dew Point (ADP) of the coil surface.

$$
BPF = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{T_{ADP} - T_1}
$$

**Significance:**
1. Lower BPF → more effective coil → better dehumidification.
2. BPF depends on coil rows, face velocity, and fin spacing.
3. An ideal coil has BPF = 0; a *very short* coil has BPF → 1.

*(3 marks: 1 for formula, 2 for significance)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice Format

---

### QUESTION A — (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a) Describe with neat sketch the various components of a supply ventilation system. List the function of each component.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The supply ventilation system consists of the following sequential devices:

1. **Air Intake (Louver + Screen):** Allows entry of outdoor air while preventing ingress of rain, leaves, and birds. Face velocity limited to ≤ 2.5 m/s.

2. **Damper:** A louvered control device that regulates the volume of fresh/recirculated air entering the AHU.

3. **Filters (Pre + Fine + HEPA):** Remove airborne particulates in stages. Pre-filter (G4) for coarse dust, fine filter (F7) for fine dust, HEPA (H13) for sub-micron particles at ≥ 99.97% efficiency at 0.3 μm.

4. **Cooling Coil:** Either direct expansion (DX) or chilled water (CHW at 6–12 °C). Removes both sensible and latent heat.

5. **Heating Coil:** Hot water (80/60 °C) or steam. Used for reheating to control dew point.

6. **Eliminator / Moisture Separator:** Removes entrained moisture droplets from the airstream after the cooling coil.

7. **Supply Fan:** Centrifugal backward-inclined (or forward-curved for clean air) fan, sized for system resistance.

8. **Duct Network:** GI sheet metal ducts sized by equal-friction method; carries air from AHU to conditioned spaces.

9. **Volume Control Damper (VCD):** Balances airflow to each terminal.

10. **Diffusers & Grilles:** Terminal devices distributing air into the room with proper throw, drop, and noise criterion.

**Sketch** (description): Block diagram from *Atmosphere → Louver → Damper → Filter → Coil → Fan → Duct → VCD → Diffuser → Room*.

*(7 marks: 4 for list with functions, 2 for sketch, 1 for working principle)*

---

**(b) A centrifugal fan delivers 5 m³/s of air at 40 mm WC static pressure when running at 1000 rpm and absorbing 7.5 kW. The impeller diameter is 750 mm. Calculate the flow, pressure, and power if the speed is changed to 1500 rpm with the same diameter.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Given:
- $Q_1 = 5 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
- $P_1 = 40 \text{ mm WC}$
- $N_1 = 1000 \text{ rpm}$
- $P_{w1} = 7.5 \text{ kW}$
- $D_1 = D_2 = 750 \text{ mm}$
- $N_2 = 1500 \text{ rpm}$

#### Step 1 — New Volume Flow (Fan Law #1)

$$
\frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1} \Rightarrow Q_2 = 5 \times \frac{1500}{1000}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_2 = 7.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}}
$$

#### Step 2 — New Static Pressure (Fan Law #2)

$$
\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2 \Rightarrow P_2 = 40 \times \left(\frac{1500}{1000}\right)^2 = 40 \times 2.25
$$

$$
\boxed{P_2 = 90 \text{ mm WC}}
$$

#### Step 3 — New Power (Fan Law #3)

$$
\frac{P_{w2}}{P_{w1}} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3 \Rightarrow P_{w2} = 7.5 \times (1.5)^3 = 7.5 \times 3.375
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{w2} = 25.31 \text{ kW}}
$$

#### Result Table:

| Parameter | Original | New |
|---|---|---|
| Flow (m³/s) | 5.0 | 7.5 |
| Pressure (mm WC) | 40 | 90 |
| Power (kW) | 7.5 | 25.31 |

*(7 marks: 2 for fan law identification, 4 for three numerical results, 1 for physical insight on cubic power relation)*

---

### QUESTION B — (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) Explain the working principle of a HEPA filter with a labelled sketch. What is MPPS and why is it chosen as the standard for rating HEPA filters?** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Working Principle:

A **HEPA (High-Efficiency Particulate Air) filter** is a *pleated* mechanical air filter made of randomly arranged **borosilicate microfibers** (diameter 0.5–2 μm) bonded into a sheet, folded into a zig-zag mat with corrugated aluminium separators, and sealed in a rigid frame (wood, GI, or aluminium).

Air is forced through the filter mat. Particles are trapped by **four simultaneous mechanisms**:

1. **Inertial Impaction** – Large (> 1 μm) particles cannot follow the air streamlines around the fibres and impact them.
2. **Interception** – Medium-sized (0.5–1 μm) particles follow streamlines but touch a fibre due to their finite radius.
3. **Diffusion (Brownian)** – Sub-micron (< 0.5 μm) particles undergo random motion and collide with fibres.
4. **Electrostatic Attraction** – Some filters carry static charge that attracts oppositely charged particles.

#### MPPS (Most Penetrating Particle Size):

The filter efficiency is **lowest** at a particle size of approximately **0.3 μm**, known as **MPPS**.

**Why MPPS is the Standard:**
- Particles **larger than 0.3 μm** are caught efficiently by impaction + interception.
- Particles **smaller than 0.3 μm** are caught by diffusion (Brownian motion).
- At **0.3 μm** neither mechanism is dominant — hence the filter is "worst-case" tested at this size.
- By quoting efficiency at MPPS, manufacturers provide a *conservative* guarantee.

#### Labelled Sketch (description):
Frame → Pleated Filter Media → Corrugated Separator → Gasket → Air Inlet (Dirty) → Outlet (Clean) → ΔP gauge.

*(7 marks: 3 for working principle, 2 for mechanisms, 2 for MPPS explanation)*

---

**(b) An AHU cooling coil has entering air at 32 °C DBT and 24 °C WBT. The coil ADP is 10 °C and the leaving air DBT is 17 °C. Calculate (i) Bypass Factor (ii) leaving air humidity ratio (iii) SHR if latent heat corresponds to 0.0018 kg/kg moisture removal. Take h_fg = 2501 kJ/kg.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### (i) Bypass Factor

$$
BPF = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{T_{ADP} - T_1} = \frac{17 - 32}{10 - 32} = \frac{-15}{-22}
$$

$$
\boxed{BPF = 0.682}
$$

#### (ii) Leaving Air Humidity Ratio

From psychrometric chart: at 32 °C DBT / 24 °C WBT → $W_1 \approx 0.0160$ kg/kg.
At 10 °C saturation → $W_3 \approx 0.0076$ kg/kg.

$$
W_2 = W_3 + BPF \cdot (W_1 - W_3) = 0.0076 + 0.682 \times (0.0160 - 0.0076)
$$

$$
W_2 = 0.0076 + 0.682 \times 0.0084 = 0.0076 + 0.00573
$$

$$
\boxed{W_2 = 0.01333 \text{ kg/kg}}
$$

#### (iii) Sensible Heat Ratio

Sensible heat per kg:

$$
q_s = c_p \cdot (T_1 - T_2) = 1.005 \times (32 - 17) = 1.005 \times 15 = 15.075 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

Latent heat per kg:

$$
q_l = h_{fg} \cdot \Delta W = 2501 \times 0.0018 = 4.502 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

Total heat:

$$
q_{total} = 15.075 + 4.502 = 19.577 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

$$
SHR = \frac{q_s}{q_{total}} = \frac{15.075}{19.577}
$$

$$
\boxed{SHR = 0.770}
$$

*(7 marks: 2 for BPF, 2 for W₂, 2 for SHR, 1 for final interpretation)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign error in BPF:** Always remember $(T_2 - T_1)$ and $(T_{ADP} - T_1)$ must carry the *same sign* — both are negative in cooling, so BPF is positive. Students often forget the negative signs and get a *negative* BPF.
> 2. **Fan Law wrong variable:** Mixing up $Q$ (∝ N), $P$ (∝ N²), $P_w$ (∝ N³). A wrong exponent loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Duct diameter units:** Pressure must be in **Pa** (or mm WC converted via $\times 9.81$) in the friction formula; mixing units yields a wrong diameter by an order of magnitude.
> 4. **Confusing HEPA with ULPA:** HEPA = 99.97% at 0.3 μm; ULPA = 99.9995% at 0.1–0.2 μm. A 1-mark loss per mismatch.
> 5. **Forgetting the elbow equivalent length:** In duct design, never forget to add the dynamic-loss coefficient of fittings ($K \cdot v^2/2$) to the straight-run friction loss.
> 6. **Grille vs Diffuser confusion:** Grilles are typically *return* devices (louvered face, no induction); diffusers are *supply* devices designed for induction and throw. A wrong pairing costs 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 3 – Supply System: One-Page Revision**

- [ ] A **Supply System** maintains a *positive* room pressure of **+12.5 to +25 Pa** above ambient.
- [ ] The supply chain order: **Intake → Filter → Coil → Fan → Duct → VCD → Diffuser → Room**.
- [ ] **HEPA efficiency** = **99.97%** at MPPS **0.3 μm**; ULPA = **99.9995%** at **0.1–0.2 μm**.
- [ ] **Filter weight efficiency** $\eta_w = (W_{in} - W_{out}) / W_{in} \times 100$.
- [ ] **Cooling coil BPF** formula: $BPF = (T_2 - T_1)/(T_{ADP} - T_1)$. Lower BPF ⇒ better coil.
- [ ] **SHR** = $Q_s / (Q_s + Q_l)$; if coil surface $T < T_{dp,air}$, dehumidification occurs.
- [ ] **Fan Laws** when $D$ is unchanged: $Q \propto N$, $P \propto N^2$, $P_w \propto N^3$.
- [ ] **Centrifugal BI fans** are preferred for clean-air AHU; **radial-blade fans** for dust-laden air.
- [ ] **Equal-friction method** keeps $\Delta P_f/L = 0.08$–$0.10$ mm WC/m.
- [ ] **Duct hydraulic diameter** $D_h = 2ab/(a+b)$ for a rectangular duct of dimensions $a \times b$.
- [ ] **Diffuser throw** is the distance to where velocity drops to **0.25 m/s** (50 fpm).
- [ ] **NC levels:** offices ≤ 35; hospitals ≤ 30; libraries ≤ 30; auditoriums ≤ 25.
- [ ] Standard **CHW temperatures**: **6 °C supply / 12 °C return**; **HW**: **80 °C supply / 60 °C return**.
- [ ] **Standard air density** at STP: **1.2 kg/m³** (use 1.293 kg/m³ for exact NTP).
- [ ] **Velocity at louvers** ≤ **2.5 m/s** to avoid rain entrainment.
- [ ] Always add the **dynamic loss of fittings** (K-factors) to the straight-run duct friction loss.
- [ ] **Induction ratio** for ceiling diffusers: typically **3:1 to 5:1** at terminal end.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exhaust System: General Exhaust Systems, Removal of Pollutants and Contaminated Air, Air Cleaning Devices
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET631 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Exhaust System: General Exhaust Systems, Removal of Pollutants and Contaminated Air, Air Cleaning Devices

# 🏭 Exhaust Systems, Pollutant Removal & Air Cleaning Devices

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Exhaust System** is an engineered network of ducts, fans, hoods, dampers, and air-cleaning devices designed to capture, convey, and discharge contaminated, stale, or hot air from a building, industrial process, or occupied space to the outside atmosphere in a controlled manner. It is the **"out-bound counterpart"** of the supply air distribution system and is essential for maintaining indoor air quality (IAQ), occupational health, fire safety, and energy-efficient HVAC operation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:**
> *"An exhaust system is a combination of equipment designed to remove air from a space by suction, filter or clean it as required, and discharge the cleaned or contaminated air to a safe location outside the occupied zone."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lungs of the Building"

Imagine a building is like a **human body**. Just as the lungs inhale fresh oxygen and exhale carbon dioxide, a building needs to **inhale** conditioned fresh air (Supply System) and **exhale** stale, contaminated air (Exhaust System). If a person only inhales but never exhales, carbon dioxide builds up and suffocates them — similarly, a building without an exhaust system suffers from pollutant buildup, sick-building syndrome (SBS), and occupant discomfort.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:**
> - A **kitchen chimney** = Local Exhaust System (captures smoke at the stove itself)
> - A **bathroom exhaust fan** = General Exhaust System (dilutes moisture and odors from the entire bathroom)
> - A **factory smokestack** with scrubbers = Industrial Exhaust with Air Cleaning Devices

### 1.3 Classification of Exhaust Systems

| Type | Capture Method | Typical Application | Pollutant Removed |
|------|---------------|---------------------|-------------------|
| **Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV)** | At source via hood | Welding, soldering, lab fume hoods | Smoke, dust, fumes, vapors |
| **General Exhaust Ventilation** | Dilution of room air | Toilets, kitchens, parking garages | Moisture, odors, CO |
| **Process Exhaust** | Direct from process | Boilers, furnaces, reactors | Combustion gases, heat |
| **Spot Exhaust** | Near breathing zone | Cleanrooms, hospital OTs | Bioaerosols, particles |

### 1.4 Need for Exhaust Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **ASHRAE Standard 62.1** mandates a minimum ventilation rate that implicitly requires exhaust.
> - Removal of **heat gains** (kitchens, server rooms).
> - Removal of **moisture** (swimming pools, laundries).
> - Removal of **contaminants** (VOCs, CO, particulates).
> - Maintenance of **negative pressure** (isolation rooms, labs).
> - Compliance with **OSHA / Factory Act / NBC 2016** permissible exposure limits (PELs).

### 1.5 Key Physical Metrics Used in Exhaust Design

- **Volumetric Flow Rate (Q)** — measured in **m³/s** or **CFM** (Cubic Feet per Minute). **1 CFM = 0.0004719 m³/s**.
- **Face Velocity** of hood — typically **0.5 to 1.0 m/s** for general exhaust hoods.
- **Transport Velocity** in duct — **10 to 20 m/s** to prevent settling of dust.
- **Static Pressure (Ps)** — negative pressure in duct, in **Pa** or **mm of water column (mmWC)**. **1 mmWC = 9.806 Pa**.
- **Stack Discharge Velocity** — minimum **5 m/s** to prevent re-entrainment of exhaust.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Components of a General Exhaust System

A complete exhaust system comprises **five** essential functional blocks:

1. **Hood (Entry Point)** — Captures the contaminant at or near its source.
2. **Duct Network (Transport Path)** — Conveys contaminated air with sufficient velocity.
3. **Air Cleaning Device (Filter / Scrubber / ESP)** — Removes pollutants before discharge.
4. **Exhaust Fan (Driving Force)** — Provides negative pressure to overcome duct resistance.
5. **Discharge Stack (Exit)** — Releases cleaned air at a safe height and velocity.

### 2.2 Operating Principle — Why Exhaust Works

The exhaust fan creates a **negative static pressure (suction)** at the hood inlet. Atmospheric pressure pushes contaminated room air into the hood, through the duct, into the cleaning device, and finally up the stack. The fan must overcome:

- **Velocity Pressure** ($P_v = \frac{1}{2}\rho v^2$)
- **Friction Losses** along duct walls (Darcy-Weisbach equation)
- **Dynamic Losses** at fittings (elbows, dampers, hood entry)
- **Air-Cleaning Device Pressure Drop** (critical design constraint)

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind Negative Pressure:**
> The fan does NOT "suck" air — it removes air from the downstream side, lowering pressure. The higher atmospheric pressure upstream then pushes air into the low-pressure region. This is identical to how a straw works when you inhale.

### 2.3 Design Philosophy of Local Exhaust Systems

The **Industrial Ventilation Manual** (ACGIH) prescribes a four-step design approach:

- **Step 1:** Identify contaminant source, generation rate (g/s), and toxicity.
- **Step 2:** Choose appropriate **hood type** — enclosing, exterior, or receiving.
- **Step 3:** Calculate required **capture velocity** ($V_c$) — typically **0.25 to 1.0 m/s** depending on toxicity.
- **Step 4:** Determine exhaust flow $Q = V_c \times A$, where $A$ = cross-sectional area at hood face.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Application |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Volumetric Flow Rate | $Q = A \times v$ | m³/s | Hood capture flow |
| 2 | Velocity Pressure | $P_v = \frac{1}{2}\rho v^2$ | Pa | Fan total pressure calc |
| 3 | Duct Friction Loss (Hagen-Poiseuille equivalent) | $\Delta P_f = \frac{f L \rho v^2}{2 D}$ | Pa | Duct sizing |
| 4 | Equivalent Duct Length Method | $\Delta P_{total} = \Delta P_f + \sum \Delta P_{fittings}$ | Pa | Fan static pressure |
| 5 | Hood Entry Loss | $\Delta P_{hood} = \zeta \cdot P_v$ | Pa | Local loss coefficient ζ |
| 6 | Stack Height (re-entrainment check) | $H = 0.5 \sqrt{Q}$ (approx) | m | Dispersion height |
| 7 | Filter Pressure Drop | $\Delta P_f = K \cdot \mu \cdot v$ | Pa | Viscous filter model |
| 8 | Cyclone Collection Efficiency | $\eta = \frac{d_{cut}^{2}}{d_{cut}^{2} + d^2}$ | dimensionless | Particle cut-size |
| 9 | Scrubber Removal Efficiency | $\eta_{overall} = 1 - (1-\eta_g)(1-\eta_p)$ | dimensionless | Combined gas + particle |
| 10 | ESP Deutsch Efficiency | $\eta = 1 - e^{-kA/Q}$ | dimensionless | Migration velocity model |

### 2.5 Removal of Pollutants — The Hierarchy of Control

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hierarchy of Control (per OSHA / Industrial Hygiene):**
> 1. **Elimination** — remove the source entirely
> 2. **Substitution** — use less toxic materials
> 3. **Engineering Controls** — exhaust ventilation, enclosures
> 4. **Administrative Controls** — shift rotation, training
> 5. **PPE** — respirators, masks *(last resort)*

Exhaust ventilation falls under **Engineering Controls** and is the **primary** method of pollutant removal in HVAC.

### 2.6 Classification of Pollutants

| Class | Examples | Health Effect | Air Cleaning Device |
|-------|----------|---------------|---------------------|
| **Particulates (PM10, PM2.5)** | Dust, pollen, smoke | Respiratory, lung cancer | HEPA, ESP, Cyclone |
| **Gaseous** | CO, CO₂, NOₓ, SO₂, VOCs | Asphyxiation, irritation | Scrubber, Carbon adsorber |
| **Biological** | Bacteria, viruses, mold | Infections, allergies | UVGI, HEPA, Bi-polar ionization |
| **Thermal** | Sensible heat, latent heat | Discomfort, equipment damage | Heat recovery wheel |
| **Moisture** | Water vapor, steam | Condensation, corrosion | Dehumidifier, desiccant |

### 2.7 Real-World Utility in Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Applications:**
> - **Pharmaceutical cleanrooms**: HEPA + laminar flow exhaust.
> - **Semiconductor fabs**: ULPA filters + scrubbers for acid fumes.
> - **Hospital OTs**: 100% exhaust with HEPA + UVGI to prevent infection.
> - **Industrial kitchens**: Grease filters + carbon adsorbers.
> - **Welding shops**: Mobile LEV with spark arrestor + carbon filter.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Required Exhaust Flow Rate from a Hood

**Problem Statement:** A laboratory fume hood has a sash opening of width **W = 1.2 m** and height **H = 0.6 m**. The required capture (face) velocity to contain the contaminant is **V_c = 0.5 m/s**. Determine the volumetric flow rate that the exhaust fan must deliver.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify the cross-sectional area of the hood face.

$$A = W \times H$$

$$A = 1.2 \times 0.6 = 0.72 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2:** Apply the continuity equation $Q = A \times V$.

$$Q = 0.72 \text{ m}^2 \times 0.5 \text{ m/s}$$

$$Q = 0.36 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3:** Convert to standard units (CMH = cubic meters per hour).

$$Q = 0.36 \times 3600 = 1296 \text{ CMH}$$

**Step 4:** Convert to CFM (1 m³/s = 2118.88 CFM).

$$Q = 0.36 \times 2118.88 = 762.8 \text{ CFM}$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** The exhaust fan and duct must be rated for at least **1300 CMH** with a safety margin (typically ×1.1) → **final design Q ≈ 1430 CMH**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Duct Friction Loss Using Darcy-Weisbach Equation

**Problem Statement:** A circular exhaust duct of diameter **D = 0.3 m** carries air at velocity **v = 12 m/s**. The duct length is **L = 25 m**, and the Darcy friction factor **f = 0.02**. Air density **ρ = 1.2 kg/m³**. Calculate the frictional pressure drop.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1:** Recall the Darcy-Weisbach equation:

$$\Delta P_f = \frac{f \cdot L \cdot \rho \cdot v^2}{2 \cdot D}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the numerical values:

$$\Delta P_f = \frac{0.02 \times 25 \times 1.2 \times (12)^2}{2 \times 0.3}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the numerator:

$$0.02 \times 25 \times 1.2 \times 144 = 86.4$$

**Step 4:** Compute the denominator:

$$2 \times 0.3 = 0.6$$

**Step 5:** Divide to obtain pressure drop:

$$\Delta P_f = \frac{86.4}{0.6} = 144 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 6:** Convert to mmWC (1 mmWC = 9.806 Pa):

$$\Delta P_f = \frac{144}{9.806} = 14.69 \text{ mmWC}$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** The exhaust fan's static pressure rating must exceed this value **plus** fitting losses (typically 30–50% of friction loss) **plus** filter pressure drop.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cyclone Separator Cut-Size Diameter

**Problem Statement:** A cyclone separator has a cut-size diameter **d_cut = 8 µm**. Particles of diameter **d = 10 µm** are entering the cyclone. Calculate the fractional collection efficiency.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1:** Use the Lapple cyclone efficiency model:

$$\eta = \frac{d_{cut}^2}{d_{cut}^2 + d^2}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values:

$$\eta = \frac{(8)^2}{(8)^2 + (10)^2} = \frac{64}{64 + 100}$$

**Step 3:** Simplify:

$$\eta = \frac{64}{164} = 0.3902$$

**Step 4:** Convert to percentage:

$$\eta = 39.02\%$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** Only **39%** of 10 µm particles are collected. Cyclones are **pre-filters**; finer particles require HEPA or ESP downstream.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — ESP Collection Efficiency (Deutsch Equation)

**Problem Statement:** An electrostatic precipitator has a total plate area **A = 500 m²**, gas flow rate **Q = 5 m³/s**, and migration velocity **w = 0.15 m/s**. Determine the ESP fractional efficiency.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1:** Apply the Deutsch-Anderson equation:

$$\eta = 1 - e^{-\frac{w \cdot A}{Q}}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the exponent:

$$\frac{w \cdot A}{Q} = \frac{0.15 \times 500}{5} = \frac{75}{5} = 15$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the exponential:

$$e^{-15} = 3.06 \times 10^{-7}$$

**Step 4:** Compute efficiency:

$$\eta = 1 - 3.06 \times 10^{-7} \approx 0.9999997 \approx 99.99997\%$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** Industrial ESPs routinely achieve **>99.9%** efficiency for fly-ash and particulate matter, which is why they dominate power-plant exhaust systems.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Air Cleaning Device

| Pollutant Type | Best Device | Efficiency Range | Pressure Drop (Pa) | Operating Cost |
|----------------|-------------|------------------|---------------------|----------------|
| **Coarse dust (>10 µm)** | Cyclone separator | 70–95% | 50–150 | Low |
| **Fine particles (1–10 µm)** | Electrostatic precipitator | 95–99.9% | 25–100 | Medium |
| **Submicron particles (<1 µm)** | HEPA filter (H13/H14) | 99.95–99.995% | 100–250 | Medium-High |
| **Ultra-fine (ULPA, <0.1 µm)** | ULPA filter (U15) | 99.9995% | 150–350 | High |
| **Gaseous SO₂, HCl** | Wet scrubber | 90–99% | 250–1500 | High |
| **VOCs, odors** | Activated carbon adsorber | 80–98% | 50–200 | Medium |
| **Bioaerosols** | UVGI + HEPA | 99.9% (combined) | 100–250 | Low |
| **Heat recovery** | Air-to-air heat exchanger | 50–80% | 50–200 | Low (energy saving) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — General Exhaust System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Contaminated Space\nKitchen, Lab, Factory] --> B[Hood\nCapture Point]
    B --> C[Damper\nFlow Control]
    C --> D[Duct Network\nTransport Path]
    D --> E[Air Cleaning Device\nFilter/Scrubber/ESP]
    E --> F[Exhaust Fan\nSuction Source]
    F --> G[Discharge Stack\nAtmospheric Exit]
    G --> H[Cleaned Air\nto Atmosphere]
    style A fill:#ffcccc
    style B fill:#ffe599
    style E fill:#b6d7a8
    style F fill:#9fc5e8
    style H fill:#d9d2e9
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Air Cleaning Device Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ACD[Air Cleaning Devices] --> P[Particulate Removal]
    ACD --> G[Gaseous Removal]
    ACD --> B[Biological Removal]
    ACD --> T[Thermal/Moisture Removal]
    P --> P1[Cyclone Separator]
    P --> P2[Baghhouse Filter]
    P --> P3[Electrostatic Precipitator]
    P --> P4[HEPA Filter]
    P --> P5[ULPA Filter]
    G --> G1[Wet Scrubber]
    G --> G2[Activated Carbon Adsorber]
    G --> G3[Chemical Absorber]
    B --> B1[UVGI Lamp]
    B --> B2[Bipolar Ionizer]
    B --> B3[Thermal Sterilizer]
    T --> T1[Heat Recovery Wheel]
    T --> T2[Run-Around Coil]
    T --> T3[Desiccant Dehumidifier]
    style ACD fill:#f9cb9c
    style P fill:#cfe2f3
    style G fill:#d9ead3
    style B fill:#ead1dc
    style T fill:#fff2cc
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV) System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Pollutant Source\nWelding Torch] -->|emits fumes| H1[Capture Hood\nFlange Type]
    H1 -->|V_capture| D1[Flexible Duct\nAluminum]
    D1 --> M1[Manifold Collector]
    M1 --> F1[Fabric Filter\nBaghhouse]
    M1 --> F2[Carbon Adsorber\nVOC Removal]
    F1 --> FAN1[Centrifugal Fan\nStatic Pressure]
    F2 --> FAN1
    FAN1 --> STK[Discharge Stack\n8 m Height]
    STK -->|exits| ATM[Atmosphere]
    style S1 fill:#ff9999
    style H1 fill:#ffcc99
    style FAN1 fill:#99ccff
    style ATM fill:#ccffcc
```

## 4.4 Schematic — HVAC Pressure Relationship in Exhaust vs Supply

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SUPPLY_SIDE["Supply Side (Positive Pressure)"]
        SA[Outdoor Air Intake] --> SF[Supply Fan]
        SF --> FLT[Filter Bank]
        FLT --> DUCT_S[Supply Duct]
        DUCT_S --> RM[Occupied Room\n+5 to +12.5 Pa]
    end
    subgraph EXHAUST_SIDE["Exhaust Side (Negative Pressure)"]
        RM2[Occupied Room\nNegative Pressure] --> EH[Exhaust Hood]
        EH --> DUCT_E[Exhaust Duct]
        DUCT_E --> CLN[Air Cleaner]
        CLN --> EF[Exhaust Fan]
        EF --> STK2[Stack Discharge]
    end
    RM --- RM2
    style SUPPLY_SIDE fill:#e1f5e1
    style EXHAUST_SIDE fill:#fde0e0
    style RM fill:#fff2cc
    style RM2 fill:#fff2cc
```

## 4.5 Hierarchy of Pollutant Removal Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H1[1. Eliminate Source] --> H2[2. Substitute Less Toxic Material]
    H2 --> H3[3. Engineering Controls\nExhaust Ventilation]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**"What is a Local Exhaust Ventilation system? List any four components with their functions."**

**Model Answer:**

A Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV) system is a **specific engineering control** that captures airborne contaminants **at or very near the point of generation** before they can disperse into the worker's breathing zone or the room air.

**Four key components and their functions:**

| Component | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| **Hood** | Captures contaminant at source (enclosing, exterior, or receiving type) |
| **Duct** | Transports contaminated air to cleaning/discharge point with adequate velocity |
| **Fan** | Provides suction (negative pressure) to overcome duct + filter resistance |
| **Air Cleaner** | Removes pollutants (filters, scrubbers, ESP) before atmospheric discharge |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-line examiner's tip:** Always mention the **capture velocity** concept in any LEV answer to score full marks.

---

### **Question A2** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**"Differentiate between Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV) and General Exhaust Ventilation (GEV)."**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV) | General Exhaust Ventilation (GEV) |
|---------|--------------------------------|----------------------------------|
| **Capture location** | At the source of pollutant | Throughout the room (whole-room dilution) |
| **Capture velocity** | High (0.5–2.5 m/s) | Low (0.1–0.25 m/s) |
| **Energy efficiency** | High (small flow, targeted) | Low (large flow, dilution-based) |
| **Air volume handled** | Small | Large |
| **Cost** | Higher capital (hoods, ductwork) | Lower (simple fans) |
| **Best for** | Toxic/regulated contaminants | Odors, moisture, low-toxicity contaminants |
| **Example** | Welding fume hood, lab fume cupboard | Bathroom exhaust fan, parking garage fan |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (Choice 1)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with neat sketches the different **types of hoods** used in local exhaust systems and state the criteria for selecting hood capture velocity.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A circular exhaust duct of diameter **30 cm** carries contaminated air at a velocity of **12 m/s**. The duct length is **30 m**, and the friction factor is **0.018**. Calculate:
1. The volumetric flow rate in m³/s and CMH.
2. The frictional pressure drop in Pa and mmWC.
Take air density ρ = 1.2 kg/m³.

---

### **Model Solution to Question A(a)**

**Types of Hoods:**

**1. Enclosing Hood (Best Capture Efficiency)**
- Completely surrounds the contaminant source.
- Examples: laboratory fume cupboards, paint spray booths.
- Lowest flow required for given capture.

**2. Exterior Hood (Flange / Lip Type)**
- Placed near the source but does not enclose it.
- Flange improves capture efficiency by 25% compared to unflanged.

**3. Receiving Hood (Canopy Type)**
- Placed above a rising plume (hot gases, steam).
- Captures by virtue of natural updraft plus suction.

**4. Slot Hood (High-velocity Slot)**
- Narrow opening, high velocity, used for narrow processes like grinding.

**Criteria for Capture Velocity Selection:**

| Contaminant Type | Capture Velocity (m/s) |
|------------------|------------------------|
| Released with negligible velocity (cold process) | 0.25–0.5 |
| Released with low velocity (welding, soldering) | 0.5–1.0 |
| Released with high velocity (grinding, spray) | 1.0–2.5 |
| Hot plume (rising) | 0.25 (relying on buoyancy) |

---

### **Model Solution to Question A(b)**

**Step 1: Given Data**

$$D = 0.30 \text{ m}, \quad v = 12 \text{ m/s}, \quad L = 30 \text{ m}, \quad f = 0.018, \quad \rho = 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 2: Cross-sectional area of duct**

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.30)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.09}{4} = 0.0707 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3: Volumetric flow rate in m³/s**

$$Q = A \times v = 0.0707 \times 12 = 0.848 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 4: Convert to CMH**

$$Q = 0.848 \times 3600 = 3053.4 \text{ CMH}$$

**Step 5: Frictional pressure drop (Darcy-Weisbach)**

$$\Delta P_f = \frac{f \cdot L \cdot \rho \cdot v^2}{2 D}$$

$$\Delta P_f = \frac{0.018 \times 30 \times 1.2 \times 144}{2 \times 0.30}$$

**Step 6: Compute numerator**

$$0.018 \times 30 \times 1.2 \times 144 = 93.31$$

**Step 7: Compute denominator**

$$2 \times 0.30 = 0.60$$

**Step 8: Pressure drop in Pa**

$$\Delta P_f = \frac{93.31}{0.60} = 155.5 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 9: Convert to mmWC**

$$\Delta P_f = \frac{155.5}{9.806} = 15.86 \text{ mmWC}$$

> **Final Answers:** Q = **0.848 m³/s = 3053.4 CMH**, ΔP = **155.5 Pa = 15.86 mmWC**.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working principle and construction of:
1. A **High-Efficiency Particulate Air (HEPA) filter**
2. An **Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP)**

Compare their efficiencies and applications.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An ESP has a collecting plate area **A = 300 m²**, gas flow **Q = 6 m³/s**, and particle migration velocity **w = 0.12 m/s**. Calculate the fractional collection efficiency using the Deutsch-Anderson equation. If the inlet dust concentration is **1500 mg/m³**, what is the outlet dust loading in mg/m³?

---

### **Model Solution to Question B(a)**

**HEPA Filter:**
- Made of randomly arranged **borosilicate microfibers** (0.5–2.0 µm diameter) pleated into a mat.
- Three capture mechanisms: **interception, impaction, diffusion** (Brownian).
- Efficiency: **≥99.97% for 0.3 µm particles** (MPPS — Most Penetrating Particle Size).
- Classes: **H10 (85%), H11 (95%), H12 (99.5%), H13 (99.95%), H14 (99.995%)**.

**Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP):**
- Particles are **charged** by a high-voltage discharge electrode (corona).
- Charged particles migrate to grounded collection plates.
- Plates are **rapped periodically** to dislodge collected dust into hoppers.
- Used in thermal power plants, cement kilns, steel mills.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | HEPA Filter | ESP |
|---------|-------------|-----|
| Mechanism | Mechanical interception + diffusion | Electrostatic attraction |
| Efficiency | 99.97% @ 0.3 µm | 99.0–99.9% (varies) |
| Pressure drop | High (100–250 Pa) | Low (25–100 Pa) |
| Energy cost | High (fan energy) | Medium (electrical) |
| Particle size | Best for sub-micron | Best for 1–10 µm |
| Maintenance | Periodic replacement | Periodic rapping/wash |

---

### **Model Solution to Question B(b)**

**Step 1: Deutsch-Anderson Equation**

$$\eta = 1 - e^{-\frac{wA}{Q}}$$

**Step 2: Compute the exponent**

$$\frac{wA}{Q} = \frac{0.12 \times 300}{6} = \frac{36}{6} = 6$$

**Step 3: Evaluate the exponential**

$$e^{-6} = 0.002479$$

**Step 4: Compute efficiency**

$$\eta = 1 - 0.002479 = 0.9975 = 99.75\%$$

**Step 5: Outlet concentration calculation**

$$C_{out} = C_{in} \times (1 - \eta) = 1500 \times 0.002479 = 3.72 \text{ mg/m}^3$$

> **Final Answers:** Efficiency = **99.75%**, Outlet dust loading = **3.72 mg/m³** (well within the CPCB limit of 30 mg/m³ for industrial stack emissions).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors:** Forgetting to convert CFM ↔ m³/s or mmWC ↔ Pa → lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Misapplying Deutsch equation:** Students often use w/Q instead of wA/Q.
> 3. **Missing the hood capture velocity selection table** in part (a) answers — examiners specifically check this.
> 4. **Not mentioning negative pressure** when explaining exhaust fan working — vital 1-mark concept.
> 5. **Confusing ESP efficiency (Deutsch)** with cyclone (Lapple) efficiency formula.
> 6. **Forgetting to convert** duct flow rate to standard CMH or CFM units in problem solutions.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — High-Yield Points for KTU 2024:**

- ✅ An **exhaust system** captures, transports, cleans, and discharges contaminated air via **negative pressure** generated by the fan.

- ✅ **Five components:** Hood → Duct → Cleaning Device → Fan → Stack.

- ✅ **LEV** captures at source (efficient); **GEV** dilutes entire room (less efficient, simpler).

- ✅ **Capture velocity** for hoods ranges from **0.25 m/s** (cold process) to **2.5 m/s** (high-velocity processes).

- ✅ **Transport velocity** in ducts must be **10–20 m/s** to prevent particle settling.

- ✅ **1 mmWC = 9.806 Pa**; **1 m³/s = 2118.88 CFM = 3600 CMH**.

- ✅ **HEPA filter** = **≥99.97%** efficiency at **0.3 µm MPPS**; classes H10–H14.

- ✅ **ESP Deutsch Equation:** $\eta = 1 - e^{-wA/Q}$ — used for **particulate** collection.

- ✅ **Cyclone Lapple Equation:** $\eta = d_{cut}^2 / (d_{cut}^2 + d^2)$ — used for **coarse** particles.

- ✅ **Scrubbers** handle **gaseous** pollutants (SO₂, HCl, NH₃) using liquid absorption.

- ✅ **Activated Carbon** removes **VOCs and odors** via adsorption.

- ✅ **UVGI** is the standard for **bioaerosol** inactivation (254 nm wavelength).

- ✅ **Hierarchy of control:** Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE.

- ✅ **Stack discharge velocity** must exceed **5 m/s** to avoid re-entrainment; height ≥ **0.5√Q** m (Q in m³/s).

- ✅ Always provide **make-up air** equal to exhaust volume to avoid building depressurization.

- ✅ **ASME N510** is the standard test for HEPA filter in-place leakage testing.
